title: Simplest Wood Chaise Lounge
summary: A reclining outdoor lounge chair with a pivot-bolted backrest that adjusts against a stop block — a genuinely mechanical outdoor chair, not just a fixed-angle lounger.
description:
This chaise's whole identity is its reclining back — pivoted on a bolt through the main frame rather than fixed at one angle, so the backrest genuinely adjusts and rests against a stop block when fully reclined. Build the main frame and the back frame first, notching the main frame for the back support with a jigsaw — **2x6 lumber is used for both frames** rather than the thinner 2x4 shown in some older diagrams of this design, a deliberate strengthening update carried over from user feedback on the original build.

Add the seat slats to the main frame, then attach the legs in two rows — the first row supports the main frame, and the second, taller row up front is what gives the chaise its slight recline angle and, not incidentally, provides a resting spot for the stop block when the back is folded fully down.

**The pivot bolt connection is the one step worth doing with the pieces clamped in place rather than pre-drilled separately** — clamp the back frame against the main frame at the desired pivot point, then drill through both simultaneously so the holes line up exactly; drilling them independently risks a bolt that doesn't move freely once assembled. Attach the back slats to the back frame either before or after this pivot connection is made — user feedback on this design notes it's genuinely easier to bolt the frames together first and add the back slats afterward.

tools.note: Drilling the pivot bolt holes through both frames simultaneously.

materials.name: 1/4" bolts, 4 in, with nuts and washers
Exterior screws, 2", or galvanized brad nails and glue
Primer and paint, or exterior stain

steps.title: Build the main frame
steps.body: Build the main frame using 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, or 2-3/4" countersunk screws. Notch out for the back support with a jigsaw.

steps.title: Add the seat slats
steps.body: Attach the seat slats to the main frame, attaching from underneath with pocket holes if desired.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Attach the first row of legs, then attach the second, taller row of legs to the front. Use 2-3/4" self-tapping screws and glue with multiple screws per joint for a solid connection.

steps.title: Build the back frame
steps.body: Build the back frame the same way as the main frame.

steps.title: Drill and install the pivot bolt
steps.body: Clamp the back frame in place against the main frame at the desired pivot point, then drill through both simultaneously so the holes align. Insert the bolts with nuts and washers.
materials: 1/4" bolts, 4 in, with nuts and washers

steps.title: Attach the back slats
steps.body: Attach the back slats to the back frame with exterior screws or brad nails and glue.
materials: Exterior screws, 2", or galvanized brad nails and glue

steps.title: Add the stop block
steps.body: Attach the stop block to the main frame at the position that stops the reclined back frame.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces and apply primer and paint, or exterior stain.
materials: Primer and paint, or exterior stain

images.alt: Reclining outdoor chaise lounge with a pivoting backrest
